summary: Flysurfer's most powerful LEI kite and the best-value big air option on the market. 5-strut, 6-point bridle that twists the kite to change angle of attack automatically. Streamlined LE cuts through the wind window at high speed, Dacron + Teijin D2 construction. Named #5 best big air kite of 2025-26 by Jason Montreal.
best_for: Big air on a budget — the best value-per-hangtime kite available

sources.summary: Unbelievable value for a dedicated big air kite. The 6-point bridle delivers consistent performance across the wind window and the boost is genuinely impressive for the price point.
Jason Montreal put this at #5 best big air for a reason. If you can't justify $2,500 for Aluula, the Era is where you look. Punches way above its price.

reviews.video_title: Big Air Kite Mega Review Part 1 - Flysurfer Era 2 / Cabrinha Switchblade & Nitro
reviews.excerpt: and welcome to a plus-size kite mega review, where this time around I will be sharing my thoughts on a larger variety of kites that I've been lucky enough to have in my possession all at once. In this airplane seat belt extension sized review, we are doing an all Dacron Focus covering the latest releases from some of the top brands on the market wi
reviews.verdict: in summary, I just really, really like this kite, and it is easily in my top two or three Dacron kites on the market right now, possibly even my favorite. It is just such a nice balance of everything you want, even if it is not necessarily the top of the field in any one particular category. If you are a progressing rider only looking to put up big scores, it might not be the absolute best booster out there, but it certainly is good enough for most riders. And the same goes if you are a progressing looper wanting to throw your first loops or advance in that area. Then again, it's perhaps not t
reviews.full_transcript: Hello, and welcome to a plus-size kite mega review, where this time around I will be sharing my thoughts on a larger variety of kites that I've been lucky enough to have in my possession all at once. In this airplane seat belt extension sized review, we are doing an all Dacron Focus covering the latest releases from some of the top brands on the market with kites including the North Orbit, the Fly Surfer Era 2, the Lacuna Alpha, Cabrina Switchblade and Nitro, and the Duotone Evo SLS with some comparisons to the latest Dacron kites I've reviewed, particularly the Rebel SLS. The majority of these kites were provided by those legends at Matt Kiteboarding, so be sure to hit them up to get yours. And as usual, there will be links in the description. I was also lucky enough to get upcoming demo models from North New Zealand and the Kiwi legend behind Lacuna, Mr. Dave K. If you are less familiar with Lucuna as a brand or DK in particular, then you need to check out the excellent selection of tech casts he has done with Adrian on Kiturf 365, covering so many interesting aspects of the kite industry, thanks to his 20 years of experience. I'll throw a link in the description to those as well. As usual, I'm going to be comparing jump heights from kite to kite on each session with the server app because I find it the most consistent jumping app, not only from jump to jump, but more importantly with how you are jumping. What I mean by that is what I specifically like to know when I'm reviewing is how high I'm jumping. Whether I'm doing a board off, a rotation, or a kiloop, or even just a straight jump. Having a singular fixed point on my wrist for me means that it doesn't matter if my legs are below me as I loop or do bordos or above me as I rotate through the air. I'm going to be getting a fairly good idea over the course of the review how high I am consistently jumping on each kite. Although I do try to do a bit of different things on each kite, there are just some kites that are better for boosting, others for board offs, and others for jumping one way or the other. And the surfer is certainly for me the most consistent measure I've found for all of these different techniques. So, it's not only the best for reviewing, but for me also the best for general rider progression. So, I do recommend trying it out if you have a smartwatch. I use mine on an older model Apple Watch with a cheap uh hard strap case I bought online. Now, before we get on to specific kites, I thought I would share a few thoughts on the differences I most commonly find in these modern materials and who is going to benefit from them the most. Now, of course, I don't work in the industry, so I won't pretend I know all the technical differences or manufacturing differences between them all, but these are just some thoughts I have based on my experiences riding them. I'll break them down into three main groups for now around Alula, the brainchild kites and classic Dacron which itself has gone through a lot of tweaking and advancement particularly in the last 2 to 3 years that has resulted in even standard Dacron kites like most of the ones I'm testing in this video just feeling lighter, faster, and having noticeably noticeably more performance than any kite really before say 2022 or to the extent that by now if you are still riding a kite from 2022 or earlier, I would highly recommend upgrading. Even if it is a bit of an expense, because if you enjoy kiting to the extent you are watching this video and are getting out a couple of dozen times a year or more, it will be worth it to help you progress a lot faster and enjoy yourself a lot more while doing so, as the modern kites have so much more to offer. I actually bought my current quiver for an outrageous amount of money new in 2023, but over the last 2 years, it has still uh held up and really paid off for me over hundreds of hours on the water. So, when comparing materials, let's first look at standard Dacron in relation to the new brainchild kites that I've been reviewing over the last year or so. There is a unique and much lighter overall feel to the brainchild kites that you notice as soon as you get them out of the bag and just feel the material. Once in the sky, they fly around outrageously quickly for their respective size and are super light on the bar. I would wager that for a lot of riders, the smaller sizes under say 10 or 11 m, for example, are going to be a challenge to adapt to because of this pace. But once you do, it is a bit hard going back to standard Dacron. These kites all tend to be among the best on the market for looping as they are super fast around the loop and tend to have less pull on them through that loop than most of the Dacron and top Lula kites. They also tend to have an overall lighter feeling on the bar and a general higher level of performance than most Dacron kites out there. Although so far I haven't found they boost quite as well as the top few Dacron kites like the boosters such as the XR Edge or Rebel models. How much of this is material versus the kit's design itself and the intended purpose I couldn't say. If you are a progressing rider and looking to try any, I would suggest the best place to start is perhaps from size 9 to 11 meters, as anything smaller might be a bit much to handle in terms of a kite speed at first. Now, when it comes to a Lula, I do find that the top models do have the highest level of performance of anything on the market, at least when it comes to overall power, boosting, and hang time. But they too can be a bit of a handful to get used to, especially with their loops which can be very fast and very powerful. With these kites, they will benefit any rider just about in the larger sizes at least of say 10 m and up where their speed won't be as challenging to get the hang of and most riders will benefit from their low end at least and the power and hang time when they're boosting. So, why did I go through all of that and what does this mean for most riders considering a purchase? Well, because in my opinion, if you are still a progressing big air rider of say a beginner to intermediate level, which uh for me would likely mean you're at a level where so far you are just generally doing standard jumps, perhaps only in one direction. um you're more than likely only doing smaller rotations when the wind is lighter and are not likely proficient in kite loops yet or at least not to the stage where you're doing mega loops or rotations. And um this is an area where although you can benefit from the larger Alula kite because of its low-end and added power um and the added wind range you get, it still might be a bit of overkill. And you'd also likely struggle with these kites in the smaller sizes because of their speed and power. If you are specifically looking to progress with your looping, then in those middle sizes, you might find the most benefit in some of the brainchild models. Or if you are looking for just a lighter, friendlier ride perhaps, then the larger sizes um of these kites would work nicely for you as well. If you are looking specifically at upgrading your smaller kites for big air, say 6 to 8, even 9 m, then you potentially won't benefit as much from either a Lula or Brainchild just yet, and any of the following big air kites are likely to be the best bang for buck. Now, that certainly is not to suggest you can't adapt to those higher performing materials, but it might just slow you down for a little bit at first. One additional quick note I will add is that if you have been following these reviews for a while, you might notice that some newer models of kites have decreased in their scores versus the older models. Now, this is not because the kite is, say, slower through the loop or doesn't jump as high. It's just in relation to where the market is sitting and what else is now out there. I sadly realized the flaw with my scoring system is that when I did my first review now 3 years ago, I didn't realize I would actually do anymore. So, I didn't plan ahead very well. And what would have been a 9 out of 10 then was respective of what else I was flying at the time. So, it certainly wouldn't be a nine compared to anything brand new on the market of the last couple of years. So with each new year of kites, you have to assume these kites are advancing by say half a point to a point or so every year or two. And that's why some kites might not score as well as their predecessors. If you have some suggestions of a better format or categories for the scoring, I'd love to hear your ideas in the comments. In terms of other content, I was thinking of doing a kite tier list of all the kites I've ever reviewed at the end of the year. So, also drop me a comment if you would be interested in that. I was also thinking of perhaps doing some follow-up video or podcasts where I just answer your questions. So, if you have questions you'd like to ask about any of the kites or any of the reviews that uh I could answer in a pod, please throw them in the comments. And if I get enough to fill up, say an hour or so, I'll try to record a pod or at least another video where I answer them all. With all of that housekeeping, I suppose out of the way, let's start talking about some of these awesome new kites. Uh, beginning with one of my favorite Dacron kites of the last couple of years, the Fly Surfer Era 2. As I did a review of the Era 1 year ago, it's worth mentioning you should check that out first because for the most part, the update is very similar, but with some slight adjustments to make the overall experience more enjoyable for me. The general feel of the Kite is very similar, but perhaps a little bit lighter on the bar than the last one. Um, but it would still fall into that medium bar pressure area for me. So, more than most of your brainchild kites or lighter threest strut lula kites, but lighter than your top power monsters like the Rebels or XRS. When piloting the kite around, the response is very good. Although there is, of course, a slight difference in response time uh through the bar from this kite on 23 m lines compared to most of the 9 m kites I'm reviewing here on 22 m lines. But the this kite's speed and responsiveness does tend to make up for a lot of it in comparison. I have to say that I do ride 24 meters a lot more now on my bigger kites than I used to when I tested the first iteration of the Era. So I also think I'm a little bit more used to the added length by now. I find this kite very easy to track around the window. And the weight of the bar pressure with the line lengths seems a very nice balance with how fast the kite flies. Although I certainly was overcending a bit as I adapted to it, I didn't find it quite as a challenging to get used to um as the other material-based kites like the brainchild ones. Um, and I also didn't find it as challenging to adapt to as some of the other kites in the later review, like the newer version of the North Orbit. In terms of boosting and hang time, this seems to be where the kite has done the most improvement over the first model. With the kite's design and its pulleys, you can really absorb a lot of power and wind and feel fairly in control um as you build up line tension as the kite uh gets ready to send to 12. This kite is wonderfully fast at getting up there and accelerating you off the water. And to me feels like one of the faster Dacron kites for particularly this initial part of the jump and just how much power you can hold in it. Although liftier than the first era, the flight itself is still not quite on the level of lift as the top few boosters like the Rebel or XR, but it once again doesn't feel too far off. Um, when riding it with the Brainchild kites, I wasn't jumping too far off the height of the peak, but always just a shade below it in each session. At least for my skill level, I tended to find this kite was amazing for holding down a lot of power, as I mentioned, and of course shooting you off the water, but not quite as generous with its ability to catch every gust um as you would like and expect from those top top level boosters, which just makes it a little bit more technical for boosting to the same heights as those kites. So, possibly just a little less potential boosting there for most riders like myself. Not being as naturally lifty as the top kites means the hang time, although solid, is still a shade behind them as well. Although, when I did do a good job uh with my kite management and flight, I was able to do some good board offs, which is always still a little challenging for me on a 9 m kite that isn't a Lula, depending on, of course, what the wind is doing. When you move on to looping, the kite still shines once again, but I did get the feeling there was a little bit more pull in this new model's loops, and the kite wasn't quite looping as tightly as the first one. Now, I didn't mind this at all, and with the kite speed, I didn't find the loops very intimidating at all. Um, I always felt confident enough to send them in both directions, even within a jump or two of each session that I rode it. It's still one of the top looping Dacron kites on the market. Right up there with the Nitro or Orbit, but perhaps just ever so slightly below them as there is a little bit more power and bar pressure in this kite. With this kite's focus moving a little bit more to power and hang time, I have to wonder if we will see their team riders using the newer model for big singles in competitions, but possibly going back to the first era for their double loops. Now, in terms of confidence boosting, naturally, if you add more power, you'll likely add more fun, but it will also usually make things a little bit more intimidating. However, the general nature of this kite is still very easy to handle. So, I would give it the same marks to the last model despite the increases in overall performance. So, in summary, I just really, really like this kite, and it is easily in my top two or three Dacron kites on the market right now, possibly even my favorite. It is just such a nice balance of everything you want, even if it is not necessarily the top of the field in any one particular category. If you are a progressing rider only looking to put up big scores, it might not be the absolute best booster out there, but it certainly is good enough for most riders. And the same goes if you are a progressing looper wanting to throw your first loops or advance in that area. Then again, it's perhaps not the very best for purely that, but it's definitely in the conversation. And again, it's friendly enough that you won't have too many issues there either. With all of that combined, that makes the performance across the board one of the best on the market. Now, with one great kite out of the way, next up, let's talk about a couple of Cabrina kites that fall into the Big Air and Big Air crossover categories with the most recent Switchblade and the new Nitro. So, if I'm honest, it was a little bit confusing at first that Cababrina have two powerful five strut kites that are both quite good for big air as well as a three strike free ride kite in the moto, which you would think covers a little bit of what the Switchblade is for um now that the Nitro is out there. But each of these kites is a lot of fun and quite different. So, it is worth talking about them both and who they might be for. If you look at the Nitro, the marketing just seems to be focused on big air and big loops. And from my experiences on both the previous models, I which I really loved, you can see why. The Switchblade, on the other hand, to me has always been marketed um as more of a free ride with a more freestyle focused and unhooking uh style marketing particularly in mind. um with the riders hitting a lot of kickers and doing handle passes in the videos. I've never actually flown an uh Switchblade before, so this was my first time on one. Um I don't think, however, I have unhooked in about 3 or 4 years or more, so I can't pretend I'm the guy to be talking about how good the Switchblade is for this, at least versus the current narrower field of freestyle kites out there right now. But it is fairly stable in the air and I did notice some of the DNA uh in both the boosting and looping aspects of the kite which I will go into shortly. For the Nitro in general, it is a bit hard to say too much different um about this most recent version compared to the previous model because I did ride them over a year apart. But luckily, my friend who has been riding the second version of the Nitro uh for the last year or so um did agree that the kite was fairly similar as a whole um as the last model. As you might expect, there are some slight tweaks to its stability and general feel, but luckily without any loss in the looping or boosting performance, which is great, but for the most part, if you watch that last review, you're pretty much getting the same overall kite info. It's a really solid kite for everything big air related with the focus being on looping and confidence building in that area. However, its weaker point versus the field just like the last model would be in terms of its general boosting and hang time. When we look at the Switchblade by comparison, the kite is noticeably slower than the Nitro, but still moves around well for a kite marketed for having good freestyle performance. Although similar, both have a lighter to medium level of pressure on the bar. The Switchblade doesn't quite react as quickly as the Nitro, particularly in the air, which means it is not as high performance for loops, of course. Uh, but in terms of boosting, it's a bit hard to give the edge to either kite here because the Nitro reacts faster and shoots to 12 quicker, pulling you off the water more aggressively. But the switchblade tends to have a bigger sweet spot and tends to give you a little bit more lift as you ascend. So in each session, although I tended to jump higher on the Nitro, I could see a lot of riders, particularly less experienced ones, finding the Switchblade just a little bit easier to gain height on. The same was the case in terms of the hang time where the Nitro required more active piloting to stay in the air and get some time for board offs whereas the Switchblade allowed you to just park it a bit more and let the kite give you that little bit of extra time to do your tricks. I wouldn't rate either as highly in boosting potential as the Arrow that we just talked about. Uh, but like versus the Nitro, the Switchblade would likely be easier for less experienced riders to improve their jumping and probably has more easily accessible hang time. When it comes to looping, the Nitro is still probably for me the benchmark for kite loop progression in a Dacron kite. With the new tweaks made to the latest Orbit, which I will be covering later, it has probably separated itself a little bit more from the Orbit by default, just by staying more similar to its predecessors than the Orbit has, which I find makes the Nitro just a little easier to control and a little more forgiving at the cost of slightly less performance, mind you, again, which I'll cover in the Orbit review. Similarly to the previous model, the loops are super fast and flow smoothly through the loop in a high and tight shape that makes them have very little pull, making them not very intimidating to progress with with your looping. The Switchblade, by comparison, is not as good for progressing with looping, but it is still fairly decent option as far as crossover kites go. The kite does move quite well around the window through the loop, but you do have to be more mindful to pull the bar in more continuously to get it around, and it is not as quick to engage with its recovery and shoot back up to 12 to catch you. This brief pause and slow ascent after the loop could certainly catch out progressing loopers if they're not careful, as it did to each of us at times. It will certainly loop well enough if you know what you are doing, but might need a bit more height for progression, which can certainly be more intimidating to do your first loops. With pretty much every Dacron kite, if you if you invest some stat points into hang time or boosting, it's likely going to come at a cost of looping. I would have to say of the kites I'm testing in this video along with the Duo Tone Evo SLS, the Switchblade will work the best as a crossover freeide kite for multiple disciplines and a little less purely bigger focus. Uh although they between the two of them are very different kites to me, I'll discuss a little bit more about what I think the Evo SLS will cover a bit better. Um, as far as confidence building goes, you'd give the edge, of course, to the Nitro for looping, but probably for general riding, progression, and jumping to the Switchblade. structured_review.summary: Current #1 on the WOO leaderboard despite being non-Aluula. Incredibly fast, easy to loop, and the best value big air kite on the market. The speed is a double-edged sword though - you can overfly loops until you calibrate to it.
pros: Extremely fast looping with tight, reliable loops
Best value big air kite available (non-Aluula price)
Forgiving with a large sweetspot for boosting
Excellent range - can cruise or go mental at your choice
Great power dump when sheeting out
cons: Lacks the constant forward drive of Aluula/Brainchild kites
Easy to overfly loops due to sheer speed until calibrated
Second heliloop can be weak if timing is off
Requires solid riding technique for optimal takeoff positioning
structured_review.best_for: Riders who want proven big air performance without Aluula pricing, and value versatility
structured_review.not_for: Riders wanting everything handed to them on a plate, or those who need maximum forward drive on takeoff
structured_review.rec_blurb: WOO #1 without Aluula. Fastest looping kite available with incredible value for the performance.
